# Introduction

In any manufacturing facility, equipment does not last forever. Machines wear down, parts degrade, and eventually things break. When a critical machine fails unexpectedly, the consequences extend far beyond that single piece of equipment — especially when machines are linked together in production chains where the output of one feeds directly into the next. A single upstream failure can halt an entire line, turning what might have been a routine repair into hours or even days of lost production. Preventive maintenance exists to avoid exactly this scenario by servicing equipment before it fails, but deciding when to perform that maintenance is a surprisingly difficult problem. Service a machine too early and you waste useful remaining life. Service it too late and you risk a failure that cascades through the entire production chain. Multiply this decision across dozens of interconnected machines, each degrading at different rates with different failure characteristics, and the scheduling problem quickly becomes computationally intractable for human planners working with spreadsheets and intuition.

MaintAlign is a preventive maintenance scheduling optimization system designed to solve this problem. Built on Google OR-Tools CP-SAT constraint programming solver, MaintAlign produces maintenance schedules that minimize total expected cost by jointly reasoning about equipment degradation modeled through Weibull failure distributions, production chain dependencies where upstream failures propagate downstream, and opportunistic maintenance grouping where stopping one machine creates a window to efficiently service others. Unlike existing approaches that either treat machines independently or sacrifice computational tractability to model dependencies, MaintAlign brings these concerns together in a single, solvable optimization framework. The system uses precomputed Weibull cost tables to give the solver a mathematically precise picture of how failure risk compounds over time for each machine, and it encodes production chain topology directly into the constraint model so the optimizer understands the true system-wide cost of every scheduling decision. To ensure that optimized schedules hold up under real-world uncertainty, MaintAlign includes a Monte Carlo simulation validation pipeline that tests schedules against thousands of stochastic failure scenarios, comparing performance against fixed-interval and run-to-failure baseline strategies. A Streamlit dashboard makes the entire process transparent and accessible, allowing human operators to inspect, evaluate, and override scheduling decisions rather than trusting a black-box optimizer.
This chapter introduces the research problem that MaintAlign addresses, explains why this problem matters both academically and practically, reviews the current state of the art in maintenance optimization and constraint programming, lays out the specific goals and contributions of the project, and discusses the ethical implications of this work.


## Motivation

Manufacturing facilities constantly face the tension between keeping equipment running for production and taking it offline for preventive maintenance. Every hour a machine is down for service is an hour of lost output, but neglecting maintenance carries far greater risk. When maintenance is delayed or skipped, unplanned equipment failures can cause cascading downtime across entire production chains, costing significant money and time. A single upstream machine going down can halt every downstream process that depends on it, multiplying the impact of one failure across an entire operation. In complex production environments with dozens or hundreds of machines organized into dependency chains, the consequences of a single poorly timed failure can propagate through the system in ways that are difficult to predict and extremely expensive to recover from.

At its core, scheduling preventive maintenance is a combinatorial optimization problem — it requires deciding when to service which machines while minimizing total cost and failure risk. As the number of machines, dependencies, and scheduling windows grows, the solution space expands exponentially and the problem becomes intractable for manual planning. A facility with just 20 machines and a 30-day scheduling horizon already faces an astronomically large decision space; adding production chain dependencies and the option of opportunistic grouping makes the problem harder still. Researchers in operations research have studied maintenance optimization for decades, producing a rich body of theoretical work that has advanced the field considerably. Classical age-replacement models established the mathematical foundations, imperfect maintenance models added realism to the repair process, and multi-component models began addressing machine dependencies. However, each of these advances has come with trade-offs — most commonly, a loss of computational tractability that prevents the models from scaling to real-world problem sizes.

On the industry side, the practical motivation is equally urgent. Unplanned downtime is consistently one of the largest sources of lost revenue for manufacturing operations, with some industry analyses estimating costs in the hundreds of thousands of dollars per hour for large facilities. Maintenance managers often rely on experience and intuition to schedule work, or they fall back on simple fixed-interval policies — replace every N hours regardless of actual equipment condition. These approaches are either subjective, inconsistent, and error-prone, or they are rigid and wasteful, servicing equipment that still has significant useful life remaining while potentially overlooking machines that are approaching critical failure thresholds. There is a clear and pressing need for tools that can take the theoretical insights from decades of operations research and deliver them in a computationally tractable, practically usable form. MaintAlign is built specifically to address this need by combining rigorous mathematical modeling with modern constraint programming in a tool that is open-source, configurable, and designed for real use.

## Current State of the Art

The academic literature on maintenance optimization spans several decades and encompasses a wide range of modeling approaches, each addressing different aspects of the scheduling problem with varying degrees of realism and computational feasibility. Understanding where these approaches succeed and where they fall short is essential to motivating the design decisions behind MaintAlign and to appreciating why the specific combination of techniques that MaintAlign employs represents a meaningful advance over the existing landscape.

# Age-Replacement and Block-Replacement Policies

The earliest formal approaches to maintenance optimization are the age-replacement and block-replacement policies introduced by Barlow and Hunter in the 1960s. In an age-replacement policy, a component is replaced either upon failure or upon reaching a predetermined age, whichever comes first. The optimal replacement age is determined analytically by minimizing the long-run expected cost per unit time, which involves balancing the cost of planned preventive replacement against the typically much higher cost of unplanned corrective replacement after a failure. Block-replacement policies take a simpler approach, replacing components at fixed calendar intervals regardless of how old they actually are at the time of replacement — meaning a component replaced one day before a scheduled block replacement gets replaced again the next day, wasting nearly all of its useful life. While these models provided the critical mathematical foundation for the entire field of maintenance optimization and remain widely cited in the literature, they are limited by two fundamental assumptions: they consider only single-component systems operating in isolation, and they assume that every maintenance action perfectly restores the equipment to an as-good-as-new condition. In any real manufacturing environment with interconnected machines, shared resources, and repairs that range in quality and completeness, both of these assumptions break down immediately.

# Imperfect Maintenance Models

Kijima addressed the unrealistic perfect-repair assumption in 1989 by introducing a framework for imperfect maintenance. The key insight is that most real repairs do not make a machine brand new — they restore it to some intermediate condition between its pre-repair state and new. Kijima formalized this through the concept of virtual age and a maintenance effectiveness parameter between zero and one. In the Type I model, maintenance reduces the accumulated virtual age gained since the last repair but does not address wear accumulated before that point, representing a repair that fixes recent damage without addressing long-term degradation. In the Type II model, maintenance reduces the total accumulated virtual age of the equipment by the effectiveness factor, representing a more comprehensive overhaul that addresses both recent and historical wear. When the effectiveness parameter equals one, both models reduce to the classical perfect-repair assumption of Barlow and Hunter. When it equals zero, the repair is minimal — the machine continues operating at its pre-repair condition as if nothing happened. This framework was a significant step toward realism because it captures the continuum of repair quality that exists in practice, from a quick fix that barely extends machine life to a major overhaul that substantially restores reliability. However, Kijima's models still focus on individual components in isolation and do not account for the dependencies between machines in a production system, which limits their applicability to multi-machine manufacturing environments.

# Weibull Failure Distributions in Reliability Engineering

The Weibull distribution has established itself as the standard probability distribution for modeling equipment failure times in reliability engineering, and for good reason. Its flexibility comes from two parameters: a shape parameter (often denoted β\beta
β) that controls the character of the failure rate, and a scale parameter (often denoted η\eta
η) that controls the characteristic life of the equipment. When β<1\beta < 1
β<1, the failure rate decreases over time, corresponding to infant mortality where defective units fail early. When β=1\beta = 1
β=1, the failure rate is constant, corresponding to random failures with no age dependence (equivalent to the exponential distribution). When β>1\beta > 1
β>1, the failure rate increases over time, corresponding to wear-out failures — and this is the regime that makes preventive maintenance worthwhile, because the longer you wait, the more likely failure becomes.

The Weibull hazard function h(t)=(β/η)(t/η)β−1h(t) = (\beta / \eta)(t / \eta)^{\beta - 1}
h(t)=(β/η)(t/η)β−1 provides an instantaneous failure rate at any given age, and this can be integrated to compute cumulative failure probabilities and expected costs over any time interval. MaintAlign exploits this mathematical structure by precomputing cost tables that map each machine's discrete age (in time periods) to an expected failure cost, calculated as the probability of failure in that period multiplied by the cost of failure for that specific machine and its downstream dependents. This precomputation is a deliberate architectural decision — by front-loading the expensive Weibull probability calculations into a one-time preprocessing step, MaintAlign keeps the CP-SAT model clean and efficient, allowing the solver to work with integer cost coefficients rather than performing floating-point probability arithmetic during the combinatorial search process.

## Goals of the Project

The goal of MaintAlign is to fundamentally rethink how manufacturing facilities approach preventive maintenance scheduling. Current industry practice relies on rigid, fixed-interval schedules or purely reactive run-to-failure strategies that either waste resources by servicing machines too early or leave facilities vulnerable to catastrophic cascading failures by servicing them too late. MaintAlign tackles this head-on by formulating maintenance scheduling as a constraint programming problem using Google OR-Tools CP-SAT, producing schedules that minimize total expected cost — combining both the direct cost of performing maintenance and the expected cost of equipment failure weighted by Weibull-derived degradation curves. The system models equipment degradation through Weibull failure distributions with precomputed cost tables, giving the solver a mathematically precise picture of how failure risk compounds non-linearly over time for each machine rather than relying on the oversimplified fixed-interval assumptions that dominate existing approaches. What sets MaintAlign apart from existing optimization approaches is its ability to simultaneously reason about production chain dependencies — understanding that a single upstream failure does not just take out one machine but cascades through every downstream process, multiplying costs in ways that models treating machines independently completely miss. The system also implements opportunistic maintenance grouping directly within the constraint model, capturing a strategy that experienced maintenance managers use intuitively but that no existing open-source tool encodes within a formal optimization framework.
Specifically, MaintAlign aims to:

Minimize total expected cost across entire production chains by jointly optimizing maintenance timing for all machines simultaneously, eliminating the costly blind spots that come from scheduling machines independently and accounting for the true system-wide impact of every maintenance decision.
Model realistic, non-linear equipment degradation using Weibull failure distributions with precomputed cost tables that translate each machine's age into a precise expected failure cost, replacing the naive fixed-interval assumptions that most commercial CMMS systems still rely on.
Capture cascading production chain dependencies by explicitly modeling production topology so that every scheduling decision reflects the propagated downstream cost of failure, not just the isolated cost to a single machine.
Implement opportunistic maintenance grouping within a formal optimization framework — turning an informal industry heuristic into a mathematically rigorous scheduling strategy encoded directly in the CP-SAT model, ensuring that grouping decisions are globally optimal with respect to the entire schedule.
Validate every optimized schedule through Monte Carlo simulation against fixed-interval and run-to-failure baseline strategies across thousands of stochastic failure scenarios, providing concrete empirical proof that the optimization delivers real cost savings under realistic uncertainty.

## Ethical Implications

The ethical implications of MaintAlign are closely tied to worker safety and responsible use, and they deserve careful consideration even though the project's primary focus is cost optimization. As major studies of ethical issues in computer science consistently assert, no project is completely value-neutral, and MaintAlign is no exception.

The most significant positive ethical impact of MaintAlign is its potential to improve worker safety in manufacturing environments. Better maintenance scheduling directly reduces the risk of unexpected equipment failures — the kind of sudden, violent breakdowns that can injure or kill workers on the factory floor. By using Weibull degradation curves to identify when machines are approaching critical failure thresholds, MaintAlign can schedule maintenance interventions before risk reaches dangerous levels. The production chain dependency modeling adds an additional safety dimension that is absent from most existing tools: by understanding how failures cascade through interconnected equipment, the system can prioritize maintenance on upstream machines whose failure would create hazardous conditions not just at one workstation but across an entire production line. In this sense, MaintAlign has the potential to make manufacturing environments measurably safer than they are under current fixed-interval or reactive maintenance practices.
H
owever, any optimization-based system deployed in a safety-critical environment carries risks that must be honestly acknowledged and actively mitigated.

The first concern is over-reliance on automation. If operators trust the optimizer blindly without maintaining human oversight and judgment, edge cases, sensor errors, or poor-quality input data could lead to dangerous outcomes. A schedule that appears optimal according to the mathematical model might miss real-world warning signs that an experienced technician would catch — unusual vibrations, abnormal sounds, visible wear patterns, or environmental conditions that fall outside what the Weibull parameters capture. The model is fundamentally a simplification of physical reality, and no mathematical abstraction perfectly represents the full complexity of equipment degradation in a real factory.

The second concern involves information accuracy and algorithmic bias in how the optimizer allocates maintenance resources. The quality of any optimized schedule is fundamentally bounded by the quality of the Weibull parameters estimated from historical failure data. If a facility's failure records are incomplete — for instance, if minor failures were not logged, or if certain machines have shorter operational histories — then the estimated Weibull shape and scale parameters will be unreliable, and the resulting schedules will reflect those inaccuracies. Worse, if cost parameters are set incorrectly — for example, if a machine's downstream failure impact is underestimated because the production chain topology was not fully specified — the optimizer might systematically deprioritize that equipment in every schedule it produces, creating a persistent hidden safety risk that is not apparent from the optimization output alone. This kind of algorithmic bias is particularly insidious because it looks like a rational, data-driven decision when it is actually a consequence of flawed input data.

MaintAlign addresses these ethical concerns through several deliberate design choices that embed responsible use into the system's architecture. The Streamlit dashboard keeps a human in the loop by making every aspect of the optimization transparent and inspectable — operators can see the Weibull parameters for each machine, examine the production chain topology, review the cost assumptions driving each scheduling decision, and identify machines with the highest residual failure risk after maintenance is scheduled. The Monte Carlo simulation layer provides an independent validation mechanism that is separate from the optimization model itself. If a schedule looks optimal under the deterministic model but performs poorly when tested against thousands of stochastic failure scenarios, the simulation results will expose that weakness before it manifests in a real production environment. The configurable instance generation system makes it possible to stress-test schedules under a wide range of conditions, including adversarial scenarios designed to reveal the boundaries of the optimization's effectiveness. Finally, the entire project is open-source, which means anyone — including safety engineers, union representatives, regulatory auditors, and independent researchers — can examine the optimization formulation, verify the cost assumptions, trace exactly how every scheduling decision was made, and identify potential biases or vulnerabilities. This transparency is a deliberate and essential safeguard against the misuse risks described above.
